claims.claim_text: `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` maps text into a 384-dimensional dense vector space.
claims.span_hint: line 223 | section: Worked Example: Designing the First Collection
claims.rationale: The model card explicitly says the model maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space.

claims.claim_text: HNSW is a hierarchical graph-based ANN method that searches from upper layers downward and achieves logarithmic scaling.
claims.span_hint: lines 291-315 | section: How HNSW Makes Search Fast
claims.rationale: The HNSW paper abstract directly describes the multi-layer proximity-graph structure and says the scale separation allows logarithmic complexity scaling.

claims.claim_text: The HNSW tuning table's `M`, `ef_construct`, and `ef_search` settings control graph connectivity and search breadth with recall, memory, ingestion, and latency trade-offs.
claims.span_hint: lines 317-323 | section: How HNSW Makes Search Fast
claims.rationale: The HNSW paper defines the core construction/search parameters and discusses the trade-off between more extensive search and higher recall at greater cost.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a concrete HNSW tuning example where recall rises from 0.72 to 0.86 while p95 latency rises from 35 ms to 51 ms.
claims.span_hint: lines 356-358 | section: How HNSW Makes Search Fast
claims.anchor_text: setting improves recall from 0.72 to 0.86 while raising p95 latency from 35 ms to 51 ms, the team can make an explicit product decision.
claims.suggested_rewrite: setting materially improves recall while also increasing p95 latency, the team can make an explicit product decision.
claims.rationale: These exact figures are presented as a teaching example rather than a sourced benchmark result, so they should be framed illustratively.

claims.claim_text: The workload-sizing table sets a specific breakpoint below ten thousand vectors and recommends brute force, Chroma, FAISS, or pgvector.
claims.span_hint: line 368 | section: Brute Force Still Has a Role
claims.anchor_text: | Fewer than ten thousand vectors, low traffic | Brute force, Chroma, FAISS, or pgvector | Simplicity matters more than distributed indexing. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Small datasets with low traffic | Brute force, Chroma, FAISS, or pgvector | Simplicity often matters more than distributed indexing. |
claims.rationale: This is a heuristic architecture breakpoint rather than a universally sourced threshold, so it should be presented as an illustrative starting point.

claims.claim_text: The workload-sizing table uses a 'tens of thousands to a few million vectors' breakpoint for production RAG and names Qdrant, Weaviate, Pinecone, Milvus, or pgvector with HNSW.
claims.span_hint: line 369 | section: Brute Force Still Has a Role
claims.anchor_text: | Tens of thousands to a few million vectors, production RAG | Qdrant, Weaviate, Pinecone, Milvus, or pgvector with HNSW | Persistence, filtering, and operational APIs become important. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Moderate production workloads | Qdrant, Weaviate, Pinecone, Milvus, or pgvector with HNSW | Persistence, filtering, and operational APIs usually become important. |
claims.rationale: The product list is reasonable, but the numeric breakpoint is a workload-dependent rule of thumb rather than a citable universal threshold.

claims.claim_text: The workload-sizing table uses a 'many millions with strict latency' breakpoint for dedicated vector databases with tuned HNSW and payload indexes.
claims.span_hint: line 370 | section: Brute Force Still Has a Role
claims.anchor_text: | Many millions with strict latency | Dedicated vector database with tuned HNSW and payload indexes | Specialized storage and search controls become worth the complexity. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Large high-traffic workloads | Dedicated vector databases with tuned HNSW and payload indexes often become attractive | Specialized storage and search controls can outweigh the added complexity. |
claims.rationale: This is an architecture heuristic, not a single sourced breakpoint that holds across datasets, hardware, and latency budgets.

claims.claim_text: Qdrant is a Rust-based vector database with payload filtering and Docker-friendly local/self-hosted operation.
claims.span_hint: line 386 | section: Choosing a Vector Database Architecture
claims.rationale: The Qdrant README says it is a vector database written in Rust, highlights extended filtering support, and shows local Docker startup.

claims.claim_text: Pinecone is a managed vector database/service aimed at teams that want minimal infrastructure work.
claims.span_hint: line 387 | section: Choosing a Vector Database Architecture
claims.rationale: Pinecone's official docs directly describe a fully managed vector database, but `docs.pinecone.io` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Weaviate combines vector search with keyword filtering and offers both self-hosted and cloud deployment options.
claims.span_hint: line 388 | section: Choosing a Vector Database Architecture
claims.rationale: The Weaviate README says it combines vector similarity search with keyword filtering and lists Docker, Kubernetes, and Weaviate Cloud deployment paths.

claims.claim_text: Chroma is positioned for local experimentation and small prototypes, with a simple API and optional persistence/client-server mode.
claims.span_hint: line 389 | section: Choosing a Vector Database Architecture
claims.rationale: The Chroma README presents the project as open-source AI data infrastructure, shows a minimal local API, and notes persistence/client-server usage.

claims.claim_text: pgvector keeps vector search inside PostgreSQL and supports both exact and approximate nearest-neighbor search.
claims.span_hint: line 390 | section: Choosing a Vector Database Architecture
claims.rationale: The pgvector README explicitly says it is vector similarity search for Postgres and supports exact and approximate nearest-neighbor search.

claims.claim_text: FAISS is a local/application-embedded library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors, including datasets larger than RAM.
claims.span_hint: line 391 | section: Choosing a Vector Database Architecture
claims.rationale: The FAISS README directly describes it as a library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors, including sets that may not fit in RAM.

claims.claim_text: The hybrid-retrieval section uses a specific example of retrieving fifty vector candidates before merging with keyword candidates.
claims.span_hint: line 441 | section: Hybrid Search and Re-Ranking
claims.anchor_text: For example, the system might retrieve the top fifty vector candidates and
claims.suggested_rewrite: For example, the system might retrieve a modest set of vector candidates and
claims.rationale: The exact candidate count is an illustrative workflow choice, not a universal or sourced retrieval constant.

claims.claim_text: The same hybrid-retrieval example also uses fifty keyword candidates and eight final passages.
claims.span_hint: line 442 | section: Hybrid Search and Re-Ranking
claims.anchor_text: the top fifty keyword candidates, deduplicate by document ID, then re-rank the best candidates down to eight passages for the LLM.
claims.suggested_rewrite: the top keyword candidates, deduplicate by document ID, then re-rank a smaller final set of passages for the LLM.
claims.rationale: These exact retrieval-stage counts are teaching numbers and should be framed as workload-dependent examples.

claims.claim_text: The filtering section uses a specific example of retrieving one hundred neighbors before filtering them in application code.
claims.span_hint: lines 260-262 | section: Metadata Filtering as a Retrieval Contract
claims.anchor_text: If the application retrieves one hundred neighbors and then removes all documents the user cannot access, it may end up with
claims.suggested_rewrite: If the application retrieves a broad candidate set and then removes all documents the user cannot access, it may end up with
claims.rationale: The exact neighbor count is illustrative; the citable lesson is that filtering should be pushed into the vector-database query.

further_reading.title: Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for HNSW structure, search behavior, and the recall-versus-performance trade-offs discussed throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Qdrant README
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the module's hands-on path because it documents local Docker startup, payload filtering, hybrid search, and distributed features.

further_reading.title: pgvector README
further_reading.why_relevant: Direct reference for keeping vector search inside PostgreSQL, including exact and approximate search plus distance-metric support.

notes: Code blocks, quiz items, pause-and-predict prompts, and hands-on step commands were excluded per instructions. The main citation needs are HNSW standards claims, product-capability rows in the architecture comparison table, and synthetic numeric heuristics that should be reframed as illustrative rather than asserted as universal facts.
